items.q: Why can't you see colour well in dim light?
items.correct: Rod cells, which work in dim light, are not colour-sensitive; cone cells give colour but need bright light to respond.
items.misconception: Colours physically disappear from objects at night.
items.random_wrong: Dim light damages the cones so colour vision is lost.
items.why: Students think colour is a property of light level rather than of which photoreceptor is active.

items.q: Does a stronger stimulus produce a larger action potential?
items.correct: No - action potentials are all-or-nothing; a stronger stimulus increases their frequency, not their size.
items.misconception: A stronger stimulus produces a larger action potential.
items.random_wrong: A stronger stimulus makes the action potential travel backwards.
items.why: Students assume amplitude scales with stimulus strength, missing the all-or-nothing principle.

items.q: How is a signal passed across a synapse?
items.correct: A neurotransmitter is released, diffuses across the gap and binds receptors on the next neurone, so transmission here is chemical.
items.misconception: The electrical impulse jumps straight across the synaptic gap.
items.random_wrong: The two neurones are fused so the current flows continuously.
items.why: Students forget the synaptic gap is bridged chemically rather than electrically.

items.q: Cone cells in the retina are responsible for:
items.why: It's colour vision in bright light. “monochrome vision in dim light” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An action potential is generated only when the membrane reaches:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: threshold potential. “resting potential” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: At a synapse, the signal is passed to the next neurone by:
items.correct: a neurotransmitter diffusing across the gap
items.misconception: an electrical spark jumping the gap
items.why: Correct answer: a neurotransmitter diffusing across the gap. “an electrical spark jumping the gap” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Rod cells give high sensitivity in dim light because:
items.correct: many rods converge on one neurone, allowing summation
items.misconception: each rod has its own neurone for sharp detail
items.why: It's many rods converge on one neurone, allowing summation. “each rod has its own neurone for sharp detail” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what peak membrane potential (in mV) is reached during an action potential?
items.why: The real figure is 40 mV. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
